name: Colosseum
description: Rome’s iconic amphitheatre where gladiators fought and emperors entertained the city. Walk around the arches and imagine the roar of 50,000 spectators.
kidsMyth: Legend says the Colosseum was a stage for heroes. Imagine you’re a young messenger racing through the arches, delivering a secret note to a brave gladiator before the gates open!
tips: Go early for the best photos and shorter lines.
Carry water—there is little shade around the monument.

name: Vatican City
description: A country inside a city: St. Peter’s Square, the Vatican Museums, and centuries of art and history packed into one place.
kidsMyth: Some say the obelisk in the square is a giant “needle” that pins the whole plaza in place. If it ever moved, the pigeons would lose their way home!
tips: Dress respectfully (shoulders and knees covered).
Arrive early to avoid the longest security lines.

name: Trevi Fountain
description: The most famous fountain in Rome. Toss a coin over your shoulder to “guarantee” a return trip to the Eternal City.
kidsMyth: Neptune’s sea horses are said to wake up at night and gallop through the water. If you listen closely, you might hear a splash that isn’t yours.
tips: Visit early morning or late night for fewer crowds.
Keep your phone and wallet secure in busy times.

name: Pantheon
description: A masterpiece of Roman engineering with a perfect dome and open oculus. Step inside and feel the scale of 2,000 years of design.
kidsMyth: The oculus is like a giant eye watching over Rome. When it rains, the Pantheon is “drinking” from the sky—don’t worry, the floor has secret drains!
tips: Step inside to feel the dome—photos work best from the center.
A quick stop, but worth it even on busy days.

name: Roman Forum
description: The political and social heart of ancient Rome. Walk the Via Sacra where triumphs, speeches, and everyday life once unfolded.
kidsMyth: They say the stones can still remember speeches. Pick a pillar and pretend you’re a young senator practicing your most powerful Roman voice.
tips: Wear comfortable shoes—uneven stones and long paths.
Pair it with the Colosseum on the same day.

name: Spanish Steps
description: A grand staircase linking Piazza di Spagna to Trinità dei Monti. A classic spot for people-watching and sunset photos.
kidsMyth: The steps are said to count your wishes. Climb slowly and make one wish on every landing—just don’t tell anyone or it won’t come true.
tips: Sunset is the prettiest time for photos.
Rules change: avoid sitting where it’s not allowed.

name: Piazza Navona
description: One of Rome’s most beautiful squares, built on a stadium’s footprint. Fountains, artists, and evening energy fill the space.
kidsMyth: People say the fountains are “training pools” for sea creatures learning to swim in fresh water. Look for the one that’s splashing the most.
tips: Evening visits feel magical with lights and street artists.
Great place for a quick gelato break.

name: Castel Sant'Angelo
description: From emperor’s tomb to fortress and papal refuge. Climb up for sweeping views over the Tiber and St. Peter’s dome.
kidsMyth: A secret tunnel once helped popes escape. Imagine you’re a night guard with a lantern, listening for footsteps echoing inside the stone halls.
tips: Go up to the terrace for the best skyline view.
Bring a light jacket—wind can be strong on top.

name: St. Peter’s Basilica
description: The heart of Catholicism and a showcase of Renaissance art. Inside: Michelangelo’s Pietà and a soaring dome above Rome.
kidsMyth: The giant dome is like a stone umbrella protecting the city. If you whisper a wish inside, the echo is said to carry it upward to the top.
tips: Dress code applies (shoulders and knees covered).
Go early for shorter security queues.

name: Vatican Museums
description: A vast museum complex packed with classical sculptures, galleries, and masterpieces. Plan your route to save energy and time.
kidsMyth: Somewhere in the halls, a marble statue is said to blink when nobody is looking. The trick is to look away at the same time!
tips: Book a timed entry to avoid long waits.
Pick a few highlights instead of trying to see everything.

name: Galleria Borghese
description: An intimate museum famous for Bernini sculptures and Caravaggio paintings. Timed entry makes it calmer than most big museums.
kidsMyth: They say one statue here is so realistic it could step off its pedestal. If you spot the most lifelike face, you’ve found the ‘sleeping’ statue.
tips: Reservation is usually required—book ahead.
Arrive a little early; entry is timed.

name: Villa Borghese Gardens
description: Rome’s green escape: lakes, viewpoints, and shaded paths. Perfect for a relaxing break between heavy-history stops.
kidsMyth: In the little lake, the temple is said to belong to a friendly water spirit who protects tired travelers. If you’re kind to nature, it grants extra energy!
tips: Great mid-day break from the heat.
Rent a bike to cover more ground.

name: Capitoline Museums
description: A world-class collection of Roman art and sculpture on Michelangelo’s Capitoline Hill. Great views over the Forum nearby.
kidsMyth: A bronze wolf here once guarded the hill at night. If you hear a faint ‘clink’, it might be the wolf’s armor as it patrols.
tips: Combine with Capitoline Hill viewpoints.
Take your time—this museum rewards slow looking.

name: Vittoriano (Altare della Patria)
description: A monumental white-marble complex honoring the unification of Italy. The terrace offers one of the best panoramic viewpoints in central Rome.
kidsMyth: At night, the giant statues are said to ‘wake up’ and watch over the city. They stand perfectly still—but they’re always counting the stars.
tips: Go up to the terrace for panoramic views.
Best photos with a wide-angle lens.

name: Piazza del Popolo
description: A grand northern gateway to the historic center. Nearby viewpoints at Pincio Hill are perfect for golden-hour photos.
kidsMyth: The twin churches are like two ‘watch towers’ for friendly guardian spirits. They help travelers find the right road into the city.
tips: Walk up to Pincio Terrace for a sunset view.
Good starting point for a stroll into the center.

name: Circus Maximus
description: The site of ancient Rome’s massive chariot-racing stadium. Today it’s an open space, but its scale is still easy to feel.
kidsMyth: The fastest chariot team was said to be blessed by the wind god. If you run here and feel a sudden breeze, you might be getting a ‘speed boost’!
tips: Best enjoyed by imagining the stadium’s size—look end to end.
Great stop between Aventine Hill and the Forum.

name: Baths of Caracalla
description: Enormous imperial baths that once hosted exercise, libraries, and social life. The ruins hint at the luxury of ancient Rome.
kidsMyth: The baths were so huge that echoes could travel like secret messages. Try clapping—imagine your sound racing through marble halls!
tips: Bring water; the site is large and open.
Look for mosaic floors and massive brick walls.

name: Trastevere
description: A charming neighborhood of cobbled lanes, small piazzas, and lively evenings. A great place for dinner after sightseeing.
kidsMyth: Local cats are said to be the neighborhood’s ‘secret guides’. If one follows you for a few steps, it means you’re going the right way to something tasty.
tips: Best in the evening for atmosphere and food.
Explore side streets for quieter corners.

name: Via Appia Antica
description: One of the oldest roads of the Roman world. Walk or bike along ancient stones lined with tombs, ruins, and countryside views.
kidsMyth: They say the road stones are ‘memory stones’. Every traveler leaves a tiny piece of their story behind, and the road remembers it forever.
tips: Great by bike; weekends are calmer with fewer cars.
Bring sunscreen—many sections are exposed.

name: Catacombs of Callixtus
description: Underground burial tunnels from early Christian Rome. A quiet, atmospheric counterpoint to the crowds in the city center.
kidsMyth: People whisper about ‘lantern lights’ that appear when explorers get lost. The catacombs are like a maze that helps kind visitors find their way out.
tips: Bring a light jacket—it can feel cool underground.
Join a guided visit; access is usually controlled.
